does: This paper addresses fairness in personalized text generation by framing it as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem that balances reducing demographic disparities against maintaining personalization quality. The authors develop a Pareto-guided teacher alignment framework combining revision-based candidate generation, pair-aware feasibility gating, and Pareto-frontier selection to identify trade-off optimal solutions. They evaluate their approach on persuasive text generation tasks for climate change and vaccination using a systematic demographic grid with matched pairs and a comprehensive five-dimension audit suite measuring persuasion bias, formality disparity, emotional framing disparity, lexical association disparity, and personalization fidelity. The key finding is that no single alignment strategy achieves optimality across all objectives simultaneously; instead, different methods occupy different positions on the Pareto frontier, enabling users to select approaches matching their specific fairness-utility preferences.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on fairness in neural text generation, building on prior research addressing demographic bias in language models and fairness-utility trade-offs in machine learning. It responds to limitations of single-objective debiasing approaches by adopting multi-objective optimization perspectives developed in the broader ML fairness community. The paper combines recent advances in preference-based alignment methods, particularly supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization used for language model instruction-following, with classical Pareto-optimality concepts from economics and decision theory. The focus on personalized generation connects to work studying how demographic conditioning affects model outputs, while the revision-based candidate generation approach builds on iterative refinement techniques in controllable generation. The comprehensive evaluation framework draws on established auditing methodologies for bias measurement across multiple linguistic dimensions.
enables: This framework enables several future research directions in fair personalization. By establishing that multi-objective Pareto frontiers characterize the fairness-utility landscape more accurately than single solutions, it opens investigation into interactive systems where users or stakeholders can navigate trade-offs according to their priorities. The approach could extend to other personalization dimensions beyond demographics and other generation tasks requiring fairness constraints. The pair-aware gating mechanism and systematic evaluation suite provide foundations for studying how fairness interventions propagate across different demographic combinations and whether certain demographic pairs present fundamentally harder alignment problems. The work also enables development of more sophisticated preference optimization methods that explicitly optimize for Pareto optimality rather than single objectives, and points toward applications where transparent trade-off communication is crucial, such as content moderation, hiring communications, and public-facing AI systems requiring accountability to diverse stakeholder groups.
